text: During laparoscopic exploration, ink is visible in the descending colon, indicating the location of a previously marked area for surgical intervention.
The surgeon performs an intraoperative colonoscopy to visually confirm the location of the submucosal lesion within the colon.
The endoscope is positioned directly at the level of the submucosal lesion, providing a clear view of the affected area within the bowel.
The surgeon identifies the mass situated at the anti-mesenteric border of the colon, visible during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic clip applier to mark the region of the colon where the submucosal lesion is located, as confirmed by intraoperative colonoscopy.
Robotic ports are placed along the patient's right side, preparing for the robotic-assisted small bowel resection procedure.
The surgeon uses an instrument to sweep the small bowel away from the colon, creating a clear separation between these two structures.
The robotic surgical system is docked at the patient's left hip, positioning the instruments for the small bowel resection procedure.
The surgeon visualizes the area of the lesion on the anti-mesenteric border of the colon, marked by ink and a laparoscopic clip.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to make a longitudinal incision in the colon, creating a visible cut along the length of the tissue.
The surgeon continues the dissection until the lumen of the colon is clearly visualized, indicating the progression of the surgical procedure.
Thermal spread is visible around the area where the surgical instrument is in contact with the tissue.
The surgeon uses a suction evacuation system to clear fluids and maintain clear visualization of the surgical field during the small bowel resection.
The surgeon continues dissection along the medial border of the submucosal mass, carefully separating it from surrounding tissue.
The assistant uses a grasper to provide traction, while the surgeon employs a robotic instrument with cautery to continue the dissection along the colon.
The mucosa overlying the lesion appears severely inflamed, with visible redness and swelling indicating inflammation.
The surgeon observes a significant inflammatory response in the submucosal plane caused by the lipoma, with visible changes in tissue appearance.

The surgeon performs a full thickness dissection, removing the severely inflamed mucosa overlying the submucosal mass, with robotic instruments providing traction and a suction system maintaining clear visualization.
The surgeon continues the dissection very close to the submucosal lesion, maintaining proximity to the inflamed tissue while using robotic instruments for precision.
The surgeon carefully dissects the mass away from the subserosal plane, revealing a clear separation between the mass and the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon positions the lipoma adjacent to the colon, showing the spatial relationship between the two structures.
The surgeon holds the colotomy open, preparing for a planned transverse closure of the incision.
The surgeon gently dissects the descending colon away from the lateral sidewall to facilitate a tension-free anastomosis.
The surgeon places full thickness stay sutures in the colon, beginning with the lateral portion, in preparation for closure.
The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to elevate the stay suture toward the abdominal wall, providing tension and exposure for the surgical field.
The surgeon places an additional suture in the mid portion of the colotomy, securing the tissue for closure.
The surgeon ties down the suture in preparation for the planned stapled closure of the colotomy, ensuring the area is secured for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon secures the suture with several knots before cutting and removing the needle from the patient's abdomen.
The surgeon performs a third full thickness stitch at the medial portion of the colotomy, using robotic assistance to secure the suture in place.
The surgeon uses the robotic arm to grasp the suture and elevate it toward the abdominal wall, maintaining tension on the stay suture.
The robotic stapler is introduced into the abdomen, positioned to perform the planned stapled closure of the colotomy.
The surgeon uses the medial stay suture to adjust the angle of the tissue, facilitating the alignment for the planned stapled closure.
The surgeon closes the medial portion of the colotomy and fires the robotic stapler, visibly sealing the bowel segment.
The surgeon places another stay suture to approximate the two sides of the colotomy, ensuring alignment for closure.
The surgeon secures the suture with several knots, and subsequently removes the needle from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon introduces the stapler into the abdomen, preparing to continue the closure of the colotomy.
The surgeon uses an assistant grasper and stay sutures to achieve the proper angle for the stapler, preparing to fire it for closure of the colotomy.
The surgeon closes the stapler, which visibly cracks through the colonic tissue during the small bowel resection.
The surgeon places a stay suture to approximate the edges of the collotomy, ensuring proper alignment for closure.
The surgeon positions the stapler away from the inflamed colonic tissue, achieving a successful closure of the collotomy.
The surgeon uses an assistance grasper to manipulate the stay suture and anastomotic edge, achieving proper angulation of the collotomy.
The surgeon uses a stapler to safely continue the anastomosis, aligning the bowel edges for secure closure.
The video shows a small portion of the colon being excised, with minimal tissue removal visible during this stage of the procedure.
The surgeon uses stay sutures and the anastomotic edge to constantly reposition the colon, ensuring optimal alignment during the procedure.
The surgeon closes the colotomy, ensuring the closure does not cause stenosis, with careful manipulation of the tissue.
The surgeon places the lipoma and the anastomotic edge into an endocatch bag, preparing these tissues for removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon closes the endocatch bag containing the specimen and carefully extracts it from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon inspects the operative field, visually assessing the area for any signs of bleeding or complications following the small bowel resection.
The surgeon inspects the closed staple line, ensuring there is no bleeding or stenosis visible along the resected small bowel segment.
